28: A direct revelation: the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve and to give his life as a ransom for many. The ransom-saying is Jesus's most concentrated self-interpretation of his death before the passion narrative begins. He gives his psyche (self, life, person) as the lytron (ransom price) anti (in exchange for, substituting for) the many. The Isaianic Servant background (Isaiah 53:10-12) fills the content: the Servant who makes his soul an offering for sin thereby justifies many. Christ's death is not an accident or martyrdom but a purposive self-offering with the specific intent of ransoming others from what they cannot ransom themselves from.
1: A revelation of God: "For the kingdom of heaven is like a master of a house who went out early in the morning to hire laborers for his vineyard." The Laborers parable illustrates the first-last principle (Matthew 19:30; Matthew 20:16). The householder who hires workers throughout the day — early morning, 9 AM, noon, 3 PM, 5 PM — and pays all the same wage is the Christological revelation of grace that exceeds merit-accounting. The vineyard (Israel in the OT: Isaiah 5:1-7; Psalms 80:8-16) is the kingdom; the householder is the Father.
2: A revelation of God: "After agreeing with the laborers for a denarius a day, he sent them into his vineyard." The agreed denarius — the standard day's wage — is the fair transaction with those hired first. The contract is just. The Christological setup: the parable's complaint comes not from injustice but from the householder's generosity beyond the contract. The kingdom's offense to merit-thinking is not unfairness but excess grace.
3: A revelation of God: "And going out about the third hour he saw others standing idle in the marketplace." The third-hour hiring (9 AM) is the second wave — those who were not hired at dawn but were ready and waiting. The Christological pattern: the kingdom's invitation goes out repeatedly throughout the day, reaching those who waited without opportunity, those who came late, those who seem marginal to the early-morning hires.
4: A revelation of God: "And to them he said, 'You go into the vineyard too, and whatever is right I will give you.' So they went." The "whatever is right I will give you" — not a specified amount — is the promise that depends on the householder's judgment of what is right. The Christological trust-claim: the kingdom worker who enters on this basis trusts the householder's character, not a contract. This is the faith-relationship that exceeds the wage-earner relationship.
5: A revelation of God: "Going out again about the sixth hour and the ninth hour, he did the same." The repeated hirings at noon and 3 PM continue the pattern of the householder going out to find workers. The Christological persistence: the master does not wait for workers to come to him but goes out to find them. The initiative of the kingdom is always the householder's — the calling precedes the coming.
6: A revelation of God: "And about the eleventh hour he went out and found others standing. And he said to them, 'Why do you stand here idle all day?'" The eleventh-hour workers — with barely an hour remaining in the workday — are the ones who have been standing idle "all day" (holēn tēn hēmeran). They have not been avoiding work; they had no opportunity. The Christological compassion: the householder finds them at the last hour and gives them work — and the same wage. Grace finds those who were waiting without invitation.
7: A revelation of God: "They said to him, 'Because no one has hired us.' He said to them, 'You go into the vineyard too.'" The simple exchange — "no one has hired us" / "you go too" — is the Christological inclusion at the last hour. Those who were overlooked by the market are included by the householder. The kingdom's final invitation ("whoever comes to me I will never cast out," John 6:37) is this scene: the householder finds those no one else hired and gives them work.
8: A revelation of God: "And when evening came, the owner of the vineyard said to his foreman, 'Call the laborers and pay them their wages, beginning with the last, up to the first.'" The payment order — last first — is the structural revelation of the parable's point. The last-hired are paid first; the first-hired are paid last. The ordering is deliberate: it ensures the first-hired see what the last-hired receive, creating the occasion for the complaint that reveals their heart.
9: A revelation of God: "And when those hired about the eleventh hour came, each of them received a denarius." The eleventh-hour workers receive a full day's wage for one hour's work. This is the grace-economy of the kingdom: the wage is not calculated by hours of labor but by the householder's generosity. The Christological revelation: those who come to the kingdom at the last hour receive the same eternal life as those who entered at the first. Grace does not pro-rate.
10: A revelation of God: "Now when those hired first came, they thought they would receive more. But each of them also received a denarius." The first-hired workers expected more — the natural inference from seeing the eleventh-hour workers paid a denarius. The Christological exposure of merit-thinking: the expectation of differential reward based on comparative effort is the natural human assumption that the parable overturns. The kingdom is not a proportional-reward system.
11: A revelation of God: "And on receiving it they grumbled at the master of the house." The grumbling at the master is the complaint that the kingdom's economy is unjust from the perspective of merit-accounting. The Christological significance: the murmuring (egongyton) echoes the wilderness generation's murmuring against YHWH (Exodus 16:2-12; Numbers 14:2; 1 Corinthians 10:10). The same heart-condition — measuring grace by desert — appears in every generation that encounters the kingdom's excess.
12: A revelation of God: "Saying, 'These last worked only one hour, and you have made them equal to us who have borne the burden of the day and the scorching heat.'" The laborers' complaint names the perceived injustice: equal pay for unequal work. The Christological reply will distinguish between justice and generosity. They received what was promised; the last-hired received a gift. Making them "equal" is not the erasure of their labor but the revelation that the householder's generosity is not constrained by comparative desert.
13: A revelation of God: "But he replied to one of them, 'Friend, I am doing you no wrong. Did you not agree with me for a denarius?'" The "Friend" (hetaire) — the same word used for Judas (Matthew 26:50) and for the man without a wedding garment (Matthew 22:12) — is the address to those who are in the kingdom's vicinity but not fully inside its economy. The question "did you not agree?" returns to the contract: the complaint is not about injustice but about the generosity that was given to others.
14: A revelation of God: "Take what belongs to you and go. I choose to give to this last worker as I give to you." The "I choose" (thelō) is the sovereign freedom of the householder's grace: the ability to give to the last-hired what was not contracted is the mark of ownership and freedom. The Christological basis: the Father's freedom to grant grace beyond merit is the ground of the gospel. No one can demand more than what was promised; the excess given to others is not taken from the first but added from the householder's own resources.
15: A revelation of God: "Am I not allowed to do what I choose with what belongs to me? Or do you begrudge my generosity?" The "do you begrudge my generosity?" (ē ho ophthalmos sou ponēros estin hoti egō agathos eimi, literally "is your eye evil because I am good?") is the Christological diagnosis of the merit-thinker's complaint: the problem is not the master's injustice but the worker's evil eye toward the master's goodness. The envy of grace given to others is the spiritual condition that cannot receive the kingdom.
16: A direct revelation: "So the last will be first, and the first last." The principle stated at 19:30 is repeated as the parable's conclusion. The Christological inversion: the kingdom reverses the world's rankings — not to punish those who worked all day but to reveal that the kingdom's currency is not merit but gift. The one who says this is himself the first who became last (Philippians 2:6-8: "he who was in the form of God... made himself nothing, taking the form of a servant") so that the last could become first.
17: A direct revelation: "And as Jesus was going up to Jerusalem, he took the twelve disciples aside, and on the way he said to them..." The private taking-aside of the twelve for the third passion prediction is the Christological intimacy of the approaching passion: before the public events of Jerusalem, Jesus tells his closest disciples exactly what will happen. The journey to Jerusalem is no surprise — he is going up deliberately, knowing what awaits.
18: A direct revelation: "See, we are going up to Jerusalem. And the Son of Man will be delivered over to the chief priests and scribes, and they will condemn him to death." The specificity of the third passion prediction — Jerusalem, chief priests and scribes, death sentence — is the Christological foreknowledge of the exact mechanism of the passion. Jesus is not walking into an unknown fate but a known destination. The "delivered over" (paradothēsetai) is the divine passive: the Father's plan delivered through human agency.
19: A direct revelation: "And deliver him over to the Gentiles to be mocked and flogged and crucified, and he will be raised on the third day." The Gentile delivery — for mocking, flogging, and crucifixion — fills in the Roman dimension of the passion: the Jewish condemnation handed to Roman execution. The three actions (mock, flog, crucify) will be exactly fulfilled in Matthew 27:27-50. The resurrection on the third day is paired with the death — the passion prediction is always a resurrection prediction. The one who knows the cross also knows the empty tomb.
20: A revelation of God: "Then the mother of the sons of Zebedee came up to him with her sons, and kneeling before him she asked him for something." The approach of Salome (the mother of James and John — she will be at the cross, Matthew 27:56) with her petition immediately follows the third passion prediction. The Christological irony: the disciples have just heard that the Son of Man will be delivered for death, and the response is a request for places of honor. The gap between what Jesus has revealed and what the disciples have absorbed is complete.
21: A revelation of God: "And he said to her, 'What do you want?' She said to him, 'Say that these two sons of mine are to sit, one at your right hand and one at your left, in your kingdom.'" The request for right-hand and left-hand positions in the kingdom is the disciples' last bid for the glory-path that bypasses the cross. The Christological irony reaches its peak: the right and left of the king's throne will be occupied at the crucifixion by two thieves (Matthew 27:38). The places of honor requested are the places of execution.
22: A direct revelation: "Jesus answered, 'You do not know what you are asking. Are you able to drink the cup that I am to drink?' They said to him, 'We are able.'" The cup Jesus is about to drink is the cup of divine wrath (Psalms 75:8; Isaiah 51:17; Jeremiah 25:15) that he will pray about at Gethsemane ("let this cup pass from me," Matthew 26:39). The disciples' confident "we are able" is the self-assurance of those who do not yet know what the cup contains. The Christological cup is not symbolic honor but enacted judgment-bearing.
23: A direct revelation: "He said to them, 'You will drink my cup, but to sit at my right hand and at my left is not mine to give, but it is for those for whom it has been prepared by my Father.'" The confirmation that James and John will drink his cup — fulfilled in James's martyrdom (Acts 12:2) and John's exile (Revelation 1:9) — is paired with the statement that the positions of honor belong to the Father's appointment, not Jesus' gift. The Christological submission: even the Son's distribution of eschatological honor is subordinated to the Father's will and preparation.
24: A revelation of God: "And when the ten heard it, they were indignant at the two brothers." The other disciples' indignation is not moral superiority but competitive anger: the two have tried to get ahead of the ten. The Christological diagnosis: the indignation is itself the problem — all twelve are operating within the honor-status framework that Jesus will directly address in Matthew 20:25-28. The passion predictions have not displaced the disciples' competitive ambition.
25: A direct revelation: "But Jesus called them to him and said, 'You know that the rulers of the Gentiles lord it over them, and their great ones exercise authority over them.'" The identification of Gentile patterns of domination — katakyrieousin (lord it over) and katexousiazousin (exercise authority over) — is the Christological contrast-case. The world's great ones use power to compel subordination. The kingdom's great ones do the opposite. The Christological community is defined by the contrast with worldly power-structures.
26: A direct revelation: "It shall not be so among you. But whoever would be great among you must be your servant." The prohibition — "it shall not be so among you" — is the Christological community's founding charter. Greatness in the kingdom is redefined as service (diakonos, deacon/servant). The one who announces this is himself about to enact it fully: the great one becomes the servant. The pattern of the community derives from the pattern of the king.
27: A direct revelation: "And whoever would be first among you must be your slave." The escalation from servant (Matthew 20:26) to slave (doulos) intensifies the inversion. The slave has no rights, no status, no reciprocal obligation from the master. "First among you" = slave of all. The Christological precedent: the one who is first above all (Colossians 1:15-18) took the form of a slave (Philippians 2:7: morphēn doulou labōn) — not as condescension but as revelation of what true greatness is.
29: A revelation of God: "And as they went out of Jericho, a great crowd followed him." The departure from Jericho — the final city before the ascent to Jerusalem — brings the healing of the two blind men. The Christological progression: Jericho is 17 miles from Jerusalem; the healing at Jericho is the last healing miracle Matthew records before the passion. The one who heals the blind on the way to Jerusalem will himself be blindfolded and mocked by those who cannot see who he is (Matthew 26:67-68).
30: A direct revelation: "And behold, two blind men were sitting by the roadside, and when they heard that Jesus was passing by, they cried out, 'Lord, have mercy on us, Son of David!'" The "Son of David" cry from the Jericho roadside is the messianic title from the margin — the same title the Canaanite woman used (Matthew 15:22). The blind men have not seen Jesus but they have heard who he is, and their hearing produces the messianic confession. The Christological irony: the blind confess the Son of David while the sighted Pharisees plot his death.
31: A revelation of God: "The crowd rebuked them, telling them to be silent, but they cried out all the more, 'Lord, have mercy on us, Son of David!'" The crowd's attempt to silence the blind men mirrors the disciples' rebuke of the children (Matthew 19:13). Those who cannot do anything "useful" for the kingdom are persistently screened out by those who can. But the persistent cry of mercy — doubly repeated despite silencing — is the Christological faith that presses past obstacles to reach the source of mercy.
32: A direct revelation: "And stopping, Jesus called them and said, 'What do you want me to do for you?'" The stopping of Jesus — arrested by the persistent cry of the needy — is the Christological responsiveness to those who call on him. The same question he asked James and John's mother (Matthew 20:21: "what do you want?") he asks the blind men. The difference: one request seeks position, the other sight. The Christological discernment: both requests receive what they truly need, though only one gets what they asked for.
33: A direct fulfillment: "They said to him, 'Lord, let our eyes be opened.'" The specific request — "let our eyes be opened" — is the fulfillment of Isaiah 35:5 ("the eyes of the blind shall be opened") and the continuation of the messianic healing agenda that Jesus outlined in Matthew 11:5. The blind men ask for what the Messiah came to give. Their faith in the "Son of David" has identified him correctly; their request names the sign that confirms the identification.
34: A direct fulfillment: "And Jesus in pity touched their eyes, and immediately they recovered their sight and followed him." The compassionate touch and immediate healing is the Christological action that completes the Jericho scene. The healed blind men follow him — the response of faith that becomes discipleship. They join the crowd heading to Jerusalem, now with eyes opened to see what awaits there. The last healing before the passion: sight given to those who were blind, on the road to the city where the Son of David will die.
